summary: I was born in New Castle, Indiana, and that's where my family lived. My father, James, worked as an automobile mechanic there, and my mother was a housewife. Even when I was drafted, I left from the bus station in New Castle to begin my military service. After the war, I returned to New Castle and even bought a small service station there. It was my hometown and the place where my family was, so it always meant a lot to me.

After being drafted, I was sent to Fort Benjamin Harrison in Indiana on February 15, 1943. It was about 50 miles from New Castle, and we spent most of the first day there being interviewed and issued equipment. I remember my first day in the army there, pulling KP duty because my last name started with A. We stayed at Fort Benjamin Harrison for about a week before getting orders to go to different places.

I was the only one from my group sent to Fort Eustis, Virginia, after Fort Benjamin Harrison. When we arrived, the first sergeant, Jasper Haven, who was also from New Castle, welcomed me. We trained on 90-millimeter anti-aircraft guns there until May 1943. It was a good training experience with obstacle courses and drills, and I even got a short overnight pass to see my folks in Newport News during that time.

At Fort Eustis, I met my first sergeant, Jasper Haven, who was also from New Castle, Indiana. He was a plumber back home and became the first sergeant in my outfit. It was a small world meeting someone from my hometown in the army, and he was the first person I connected with there. Having him as the first sergeant helped me settle into military life during our training on the 90-millimeter guns.

During my time at Fort Eustis, we trained extensively on the 90-millimeter anti-aircraft guns. These guns could shoot up to four or five miles high, and our training focused on operating them effectively. I stayed on these guns until May 1943 before being reassigned to other duties. It was a key part of my early military training and preparation for the war.

After Fort Eustis, I was sent to Camp Stewart, Georgia, where we switched from the 90-millimeter guns to the 40-millimeter Bofors anti-aircraft guns. This was a change in our training and equipment, and we adapted to the new guns there. Camp Stewart was where we prepared for overseas deployment, and it was cold when we left from there to Massachusetts for embarkation. It was a significant step toward going overseas.

At Camp Stewart, we were assigned to the 40-millimeter Bofors anti-aircraft guns, which was different from the 90-millimeter guns I trained on before. These guns were smaller but still vital for air defense. We trained on them thoroughly before heading overseas. The Bofors guns became my main weapon system during much of my service in Europe.

I served in the European Theater of Operations during World War II. After training in the U.S., we shipped out from Boston in March 1944 aboard the USS George Washington. We landed in Wales and trained there before moving to England and then France. I witnessed the destruction and casualties of war firsthand, especially in France during heavy bombing. My time in the ETO was intense and unforgettable.

Before heading overseas, we stayed at Camp Myles Standish in Massachusetts for about three weeks in very cold weather. It was our staging area before we boarded the ship to Europe. While there, I received word that my son was born, but I couldn't go home to see him. It was a bittersweet time, preparing for deployment while missing my family.

We departed from Boston on the USS George Washington in early March 1944 to cross the Atlantic. It was a large ship, but I got seasick as soon as we left the harbor. The food was tough to eat while seasick, mostly pickles, oranges, and crackers for me. The seasickness lasted about a week, and then we arrived safely in Wales. That voyage was my first experience crossing the ocean during the war.

We disembarked in Wales and spent some time training there after arriving in Europe. It was our first stop on the continent, and we prepared for further deployment in England and eventually France. Wales was where we adjusted to being overseas and continued our anti-aircraft training. It was a crucial part of our preparation for the invasion.

After Wales, we were stationed along a main road near Puddletown in England. Our job was to guard the highway used by convoys hauling material to the sea coast for the invasion. We faced some strafing attacks from planes but no direct combat. Puddletown was a quiet but important location for protecting supply routes during the buildup to D-Day.

Our unit was stationed near Weymouth, Dorset, along the coast in England. We guarded the roads and convoys moving supplies to the sea coast for the invasion effort. Weymouth was a key location for the buildup to the Normandy invasion. We stayed there until late July 1944 before shipping out to France.

We moved to France in the latter part of July 1944 after the invasion. The destruction there was terrible, especially in the cities bombed day and night. I was stationed around airports, roadways, and cities, always on alert with our anti-aircraft guns. The war’s impact was very visible, and the casualties were heavy. I was wounded there and sent to the 99th Field Hospital in Reims for treatment.

During my service, I was awarded the Good Conduct Medal for my discipline and performance in the army. It was an honor to receive this recognition during the war. Along with other medals, it reminded me of the commitment and effort I put into my duties. It was a meaningful acknowledgment of my time in the service.

I received a Purple Heart for a minor wound I sustained in France. It wasn’t a serious injury, but it was enough to send me back to a replacement depot and eventually back to the States. The wound was connected to an eye problem that was discovered later. The Purple Heart was a reminder of the risks we faced even when not in direct combat.

After being wounded, I was sent to the 99th Field Hospital in Reims, France. It was a place where many casualties were treated, and I had my first airplane ride flying back to the hospital. The hospital was surrounded by French civilians who had very little but were generous in trading cigarettes for champagne or calvados. It was an eye-opening experience seeing the conditions there and the kindness of the locals.

I was hospitalized in Reims, France, at the 99th Field Hospital after my minor wound. Reims was heavily affected by the war, and the hospital treated many soldiers. The local French people were very poor but friendly, and I remember trading cigarettes for local drinks like calvados. Being in Reims gave me a different perspective on the war and the people caught in it.

After my injury and eye problem were discovered, I was sent back to the Zone of Interior, which is what they called the continental United States during the war. I was stationed in Chicago until my discharge in May 1945. Being back in the Zone of Interior meant I was out of combat but still serving in the army. It was a relief to be back home, even if I was still in uniform.

We returned home on the USS Lejeune, which was a hospital ship. After leaving Europe, we disembarked in Boston and then took a train to Chicago. The ship was part of the medical evacuation process, and it was a different experience from the troop transport I came over on. The USS Lejeune brought me safely back to the States after my time overseas.

Boston was the port where we embarked to Europe on the USS George Washington and later where we disembarked returning home on the USS Lejeune. It was a cold place in February when we left for Europe. Boston was a key transit point for many soldiers going overseas and coming back. I remember both departures and arrivals there during my service.

After being sent back from Europe due to my injury and eye problem, I was stationed in Chicago until my discharge in May 1945. I was able to get weekend passes to go home to New Castle during that time. Chicago was where I spent the last months of my military service. It was a big city compared to my hometown, but I was glad to be stateside.

After returning home, I was welcomed by organizations like the Veterans of Foreign Wars. They were very supportive of veterans and helped us adjust back to civilian life. The VFW was a place where I could connect with other men who had served overseas. It was important to have that community after the war.

The American Legion was another organization that welcomed me and other veterans after the war. They provided camaraderie and assistance to those of us who had served. Being part of the Legion helped me stay connected with fellow servicemen and continue to serve the community in a different way. It was a valuable support network after my military career.

While stationed in France, I remember that you could trade cigarettes for calvados, which is a strong local drink. It was a small way to enjoy some recreation despite being on duty all the time. The French civilians had very little, but they shared what they had, and calvados was part of that. It was a memorable part of my time in France.
